text.title: embedded-scratchpad-field Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for embedding a thinking field inside structured-output schemas.

rule.statement: The scratchpad field MUST be declared BEFORE the answer field(s) in the schema; autoregressive generation makes any field declared after `answer` post-hoc rationalisation, not CoT.
rule.rationale: Schema order is generation order. Reasoning declared after the answer is post-hoc; the answer was already committed without that reasoning available.

rule.statement: Pick the scratchpad field name by task shape: `reasoning` for decisions/extraction, `plan_steps: list[str]` for multi-step, `evidence: list[str]` for classification with quotation, `scratchpad` for free-form, `tab_cot` for derivation chains.
rule.rationale: The field name is part of the prompt; matching it to the task type cues the model toward the right reasoning shape.

rule.statement: The scratchpad field MUST have a `description` that names WHAT to think about (referencing other field names) and sets a length expectation; empty or generic ("think step by step") descriptions reduce the lift.
rule.rationale: Descriptions act as inline prompts. A precise scratchpad description ("Analyse each clause against the criteria above; cap at 200 words") drives a measurable accuracy lift over a vague one.

rule.statement: A single schema MUST have at most one scratchpad field; multiple competing scratchpads (`reasoning` plus `analysis` plus `notes`) confuse the model and dilute the CoT effect.
rule.rationale: Multi-scratchpad schemas show flat or worse accuracy than single-scratchpad equivalents in 50-task evals.

rule.statement: If the model is already a reasoning model (o-series, Opus with extended thinking), DO NOT add a scratchpad field — the reasoning happens implicitly and the scratchpad becomes wasted tokens.
rule.rationale: Reasoning models bill thinking tokens separately; double CoT doubles cost without quality lift.
